summary: Nadia reflects on a period of quiet exploration and iteration after stepping back from public writing. She describes the discomfort of uncertainty and not having a clear narrative for what she's working on. A friend's remark about persisting because one still has 'something interesting left to say' resonates deeply. She rejects the 'entrepreneur' label in favor of thinking of herself as an artist, committed to continuing her work regardless of timeline or outcome.
key_points: Periods of silence can be productive: listening, testing theories, and revising beliefs happen offline
The discomfort of not having a neat answer to 'what are you up to?' reflects society's demand for legible career narratives
Persistence in creative work comes from an internal sense of having something left to say, not external validation
The artist identity, unlike the entrepreneur identity, permits open-ended timelines and uncertain outcomes
money_quotes: Some of the things I felt most sure about, I've now rejected.
I keep doing what I do, even when everybody around me doesn't get it, because I feel like I still have something interesting left to say.
I'm not an entrepreneur. I never liked the label for myself. I'd rather think of myself as an artist.
Sometimes it's not clear what exactly needs to be said, or what form it will take, or even whether it will have any impact on the world.
Not knowing the future is scary sometimes. It's extremely uncomfortable to be in a period of iterating, of spending some days so elated and other days so low, sometimes within 24 hours of each other.
key_insight: The drive to keep creating comes not from knowing the outcome, but from the persistent feeling that you still have something interesting left to say.
